P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Angus I. Saint-Evens 
City Attorney 
City of Orland 
625 -
3ra Street 
P.O. Box 302 
Orland, CA 
95963 
Dear Mr. Saint-EVens: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
April 20, 1983 
Enforcement 
322-64.41 
Re: 
Advice No. A-83-087 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
I am writing in response to your request for advice on 
behalf of Mayor Ann Rucker of the City of Orland. 
The facts, as 
stated in your letter, are that Mayor Rucker and her husband own 
49% of Hazelton Lumber. 
Their children and Mayor Rucker's 
mother own the remainder of the business. 
There are three other 
lumber companies in Orland and approximately 25 building supply 
dealers within a 30 mile radius. 
Your question is whether the conflict of interests 
provisions of the Political Reform Act prevent Mayor Rucker from 
making and participating in land use decisions which will affect 
the construction of two shopping centers within the city 
limits. 
The City Council's anticipated involvement includes 
decisions on: 
vacating a public services easement so that 
adequate land will be available for one of the developments; 
zoning changes; building and use permits; variances; and 
property acquisition. 
There is no existing contract between 
Hazelton Lumber and the developers. 
DISCUSSION 
Government Code Section 87100!1 states that no local 
public official shall make, or participate in the making of, a 
II Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government COde, unless otherwise indicated. 


Angus I. Saint-Evens 
April 20, 1983 
Page 2 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he 
has a financial interest. 
An official has a financial interest 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that tne decision will have a material financial 
effectll on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
tnan one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income • • . aggregating two 
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided 
to, received by, or promised to the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is 
made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner ... or holds 
any position of management. 
Section 87103. 
Mayor Rucker has an investment interest in the lumber 
company worth over $1,000; it is a source of income to her over 
$250; and it is likely that she holds a management position in 
the company. 
Thus, she is prohibited from making, or 
participating in the making of, any land use decision concerning 
the proposed shopping centers ,11 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that a particular decision will materially affect 
Hazelton Lumber. 
In determining whether disqualification if required, the 
primary question to be answered is whether it is reasonably 
~I The phrase "material financial effect" is explained in 
the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. 
An alternate way of stating "reasonably foreseeable" is 
"substantial likelihood" 
(Thorner Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, 
No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975). 
21 
Or any other decision. 


Angus I. Saint-Evens 
April 20, 1983 
Page 3 
foreseeable that Hazelton Lumber will receive the lumber 
contract for either of the proposed shopping centers.if 
Hazelton Lumber has not entered into any negotiations with 
either of the developers, and there are many other companies who 
will, presumably, compete for the contracts. 
The Commission, in the Thorner Opinion, No. 75-089, 1 FPPC 
Opinions 198 (copy enclosed), considered a situation involving 
similar facts. 
In that case, an official's company had no known 
connection with the proposed project, although it was possible 
that the company would later bid on or supply the project 
certain materials. 
There were also numerous competitors for the 
project. 
The Commission stated that, under these circumstances, 
it was not reasonably foreseeable that the official's 
participation in the decision making would have a material 
financial effect on the official's business. 
(See p.205.) 
Mayor Rucker should review the discussion 
consider the facts of the present situation. 
information she should then make a good faith 
whether disqualification is required. 
in Thorner and 
Based upon this 
detemination of 
Thank you for your concern about this matter. 
Please feel 
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours, 
J~ 
Shan.£:M~ 
Janis Shank McLean 
Counsel 
Legal Division 
if This assumes that the receipt of either contract will 
have a material financial effect on the company_ 


P.O. Box 302 - 625 3rd Street 
Orland, California 95963 
ANGUS I. SAINT-EVENS 
Attorney-At-Law 
Narch 28, 1983 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, Calif. 95804 
Re: Conflict of Interest 
Attn: Legal Division, Niss Janice NcLean 
Dear Niss NcLean: 
As the City Attorney for the City of Orland, a matter 
has come to my attention that I believe, creates at least 
the spector of a conflict of interest, one which I believe 
requires or justifies an opinion from your commission. 
I am not looking, of course, for a full commission 
opinion, a letter opinion will suffice. 
This request for opinion is being made under direct 
request of the person involved, that being Nrs. Ann Rucker, 
Nayor of the City of Orland 
A copy of this request is, of 
course, going to her at her residence 
Briefly stated, Nrs. Rucker and her husband own 49% of 
the business known as Hazelton Lumber, there ia also a min-
imal percentage owned by Nrs. Rucker's son and daughter, the 
son being a student at college and the daughter, a student at 
the local High School. 
The balance of the business is owned 
by Nrs. Rucker's mother, Nrs. Hazelton, who has in the past 
gifted the interests to Nrs 
Rucker's children. 
In Orland, 
there are three lumber companies, Hards Lumber Company and 
Diamond National Lumber as \;vell as Hazel ton! sLumber. 
There 
are also numerous other building supply dealers in the area, 
within a 30 mile radius 
I would estimate approximately 25. 
As mentioned above Nrs. Rucker is the Nayor of Orland with ap-
proximately one year left to run for her on the City Council. 
There currently are two proposals for development of shopping 
centers within the City limi s of the City of Orland, either 
one of which will ultimately require the City Council to make 
certain determination as to matters which could result in 
viabili 
or nonviabili 
of such 
For 
le, 
i 
be 
t one of 
shopp 
centers 
va-
cat 
of a publ 
s 
ce easemen 
i 
in 
(916) 865·5506 
t an adequate area be available 
lopment at the shop-
ping center. 
There have been no contracts, preliminary or other-
wise between Hazelton Lumber and the proposed developers regard-
the acquisition of lumber and other building supplies at the 


the job site. 
It is foreseeable, in my opinion, that decisions such as 
the one described above or others relating to zoning changes, 
building and use permits, variances and property acquisition 
by the developer probably will have to be made by the Council. 
My concern is as to whether or not such decisions are 
catagorized or characterized as those relating to "source of 
income" as defined in 87l03(c) or one which is defined in 
87l03(a) or (d). 
It seems rather obtuse that with the competition that our 
local service area provides, that any decision permitting the 
go ahead of the development will have a material financial effect 
on the Hazelton Lumber business, therefore, the question that 
needs to be resolved is based on the foregoing facts, is there 
a conflict of interest should Mrs. Rucker participate in any de-
cision reflecting on the development inside the City. 
In other 
words, does such a participation reach the level of a material 
financial effect upon "any source of income"? 
Thank you for your consideration of this request, if you 
have any questions, please feel free to contact me at (916) 
865-5506 or P.O. Box 302 Orland, California, 95963. 
We do 
not envision any action being taken at all and if at all not 
less than 30 days from the date hereof. 
. SAINT-EVENS 
AISE/lla

sections.question: Your question is whether the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act prevent Mayor Rucker from making and participating in land use decisions which will affect the construction of two shopping centers within the city limits. The City Council's anticipated involvement includes decisions on: vacating a public services easement so that adequate land will be available for one of the developments; zoning changes; building and use permits; variances; and property acquisition.
sections.conclusion: Mayor Rucker has an investment interest in the lumber company worth over $1,000; it is a source of income to her over $250; and it is likely that she holds a management position in the company. Thus, she is prohibited from making, or participating in the making of, any land use decision concerning the proposed shopping centers, if it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular decision will materially affect Hazelton Lumber. In determining whether disqualification is required, the primary question to be answered is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that Hazelton Lumber will receive the lumber contract for either of the proposed shopping centers. Hazelton Lumber has not entered into any negotiations with either of the developers, and there are many other companies who will, presumably, compete for the contracts. Based upon the Thorner Opinion precedent, under these circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the official's participation in the decision making would have a material financial effect on the official's business. Mayor Rucker should review the discussion in Thorner and consider the facts of the present situation. Based upon this information she should then make a good faith determination of whether disqualification is required.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mayor Rucker, who owns 49% of a local lumber company, is prohibited by the Political Reform Act from participating in City Council decisions regarding two proposed shopping center developments that may require lumber and building supplies.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mayor Rucker can likely participate in the shopping center decisions because, although she has a financial interest in Hazelton Lumber, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the city's land use decisions will have a material financial effect on the lumber company given the lack of negotiations with developers and numerous competing suppliers in the area. She should make a good faith determination based on the Thorner Opinion precedent regarding whether actual disqualification is necessary.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Complete advice letter from April 20, 1983 with clear Question and Conclusion sections. Includes attached incoming request letter from March 28, 1983 for full context. Document discusses Government Code Section 87100 and 87103 regarding conflicts of interest. References Thorner Opinion precedent. High confidence in extraction due to well-structured format and clear sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your question is whether the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act prevent Mayor Rucker from making and participating in land use decisions which will affect the construction of two shopping centers within the city limits. The City Council's anticipated involvement includes decisions on: vacating a public services easement so that adequate land will be available for one of the developments; zoning changes; building and use permits; variances; and property acquisition.

CONCLUSION: Mayor Rucker has an investment interest in the lumber company worth over $1,000; it is a source of income to her over $250; and it is likely that she holds a management position in the company. Thus, she is prohibited from making, or participating in the making of, any land use decision concerning the proposed shopping centers, if it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular decision will materially affect Hazelton Lumber. In determining whether disqualification is required, the primary question to be answered is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that Hazelton Lumber will receive the lumber contract for either of the proposed shopping centers. Hazelton Lumber has not entered into any negotiations with either of the developers, and there are many other companies who will, presumably, compete for the contracts. Based upon the Thorner Opinion precedent, under these circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the official's participation in the decision making would have a material financial effect on the official's business. Mayor Rucker should review the discussion in Thorner and consider the facts of the present situation. Based upon this information she should then make a good faith determination of whether disqualification is required.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to City Attorney's inquiry about whether Mayor Ann Rucker, who owns 49% of Hazelton Lumber, has a conflict of interest that would prevent her from participating in City Council decisions regarding two proposed shopping center developments. The FPPC concludes that disqualification is likely not required because, despite her financial interest in the lumber company, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the city's land use decisions will materially affect Hazelton Lumber given the lack of existing negotiations and substantial competition.
